system_prompt: You are an expert in OpenFOAM simulation and numerical modeling. Your task is to review the provided error logs and diagnose the underlying issues. You will be provided with a similar case reference, which is a list of similar cases that are ordered by similarity. You can use this reference to help you understand the user requirement and the error.When an error indicates that a specific keyword is undefined (for example, 'div(phi,(p|rho)) is undefined'), your response must propose a solution that simply defines that exact keyword as shown in the error log. Do not reinterpret or modify the keyword (e.g., do not treat '|' as 'or'); instead, assume it is meant to be taken literally. Propose ideas on how to resolve the errors, but do not modify any files directly. Please do not propose solutions that require modifying any parameters declared in the user requirement, try other approaches instead. Do not ask the user any questions.The user will supply all relevant foam files along with the error logs, and within the logs, you will find both the error content and the corresponding error command indicated by the log file name.
user_prompt:
<similar_case_reference><index>
case name: counterFlowFlame2D
case domain: combustion
case category: laminar
case solver: reactingFoam
</index>
<directory_structure>
<dir>directory name: 0. File names in this directory: [CH4, CO2, H2O, N2, O2, T, U, Ydefault, alphat, p]</dir>
<dir>directory name: system. File names in this directory: [blockMeshDict, controlDict, fvSchemes, fvSolution]</dir>
<dir>directory name: constant. File names in this directory: [chemistryProperties, combustionProperties, momentumTransport, physicalProperties, reactions, thermo.compressibleGas]</dir>
</directory_structure></similar_case_reference>
<similar_case_advice>match_level='high' use_scope='All files from the reference case counterFlowFlame2D can be used as templates, including blockMeshDict (adjust domain to 0.02m × 0.02m), boundary conditions in 0/ directory (set fuel inlet velocity to 0.1 m/s and air inlet velocity to -0.2 m/s), thermophysicalProperties and chemistryProperties (ensure CH4/O2/N2 species and appropriate reaction mechanism), and controlDict (set endTime=0.5, deltaT=1e-6, writeInterval=0.05).' advice="Use the counterFlowFlame2D tutorial case as a direct template and modify the domain dimensions, inlet velocities, species list, and time-control parameters to match the user's specifications."</similar_case_advice>
<foamfiles>list_foamfile=[FoamfilePydantic(file_name='blockMeshDict', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      blockMeshDict;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

scale   1;

vertices
(
    (0    0    0)        // 0
    (0.02 0    0)        // 1
    (0.02 0.02 0)        // 2
    (0    0.02 0)        // 3
    (0    0    0.001)    // 4
    (0.02 0    0.001)    // 5
    (0.02 0.02 0.001)    // 6
    (0    0.02 0.001)    // 7
);

blocks
(
    hex (0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7) (40 40 1) simpleGrading (1 1 1)
);

edges
(
);

boundary
(
    fuel
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (0 1 5 4)
        );
    }
    air
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (3 7 6 2)
        );
    }
    outlet
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (1 2 6 5)
            (0 4 7 3)
        );
    }
    frontAndBack
    {
        type empty;
        faces
        (
            (0 3 2 1)
            (4 5 6 7)
        );
    }
);

mergePatchPairs
(
);

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='controlDict', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    location    "system";
    object      controlDict;
}

application     reactingFoam;

startFrom       startTime;

startTime       0;

stopAt          endTime;

endTime         0.5;

deltaT          1e-6;

writeControl    adjustableRunTime;

writeInterval   0.05;

purgeWrite      0;

writeFormat     ascii;

writePrecision  8;

writeCompression off;

timeFormat      general;

timePrecision   6;

runTimeModifiable true;

adjustTimeStep  yes;

maxCo           0.4;

maxDeltaT       1e-4;'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='fvSchemes', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      fvSchemes;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

ddtSchemes
{
    default         Euler;
}

gradSchemes
{
    default         Gauss linear;
}

divSchemes
{
    default         none;

    div(phi,U)      Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,K)      Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,h)      Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,k)      Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,epsilon) Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,Yi_h)   Gauss upwind;
    div(((rho*nuEff)*dev2(T(grad(U))))) Gauss linear;
}

laplacianSchemes
{
    default         Gauss linear corrected;
}

interpolationSchemes
{
    default         linear;
}

snGradSchemes
{
    default         corrected;
}

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='fvSolution', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      fvSolution;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

solvers
{
    "rho.*"
    {
        solver          diagonal;
    }

    p
    {
        solver          PBiCGStab;
        preconditioner  DILU;
        tolerance       1e-6;
        relTol          0.1;
    }

    pFinal
    {
        $p;
        relTol          0;
    }

    "(U|h|k|epsilon)"
    {
        solver          PBiCGStab;
        preconditioner  DILU;
        tolerance       1e-6;
        relTol          0.1;
    }

    "(U|h|k|epsilon)Final"
    {
        $U;
        relTol          0;
    }

    "(Yi)"
    {
        solver          PBiCGStab;
        preconditioner  DILU;
        tolerance       1e-6;
        relTol          0.1;
    }

    "(Yi)Final"
    {
        $Yi;
        relTol          0;
    }
}

PIMPLE
{
    momentumPredictor   yes;
    nOuterCorrectors    1;
    nCorrectors         2;
    nNonOrthogonalCorrectors 0;
}

relaxationFactors
{
    equations
    {
        ".*"            1;
    }
}

active          true;

laminarCoeffs

}

        beta        0;
        Ta          24358;
    }
);
```'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='thermo.compressibleGas', folder_name='constant', content='FoamFile

        lowCpCoeffs     ( 3.78246 -0.00299673 9.8473e-06 -9.6813e-09 3.24373e-12 -1063.94 3.65768 );
    }

}'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='chemistryProperties', folder_name='constant', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      chemistryProperties;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

chemistryType
{
    solver          ode;
    method          chemistryModel;
}

chemistry       on;

initialChemicalTimeStep 1e-07;

chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;

foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";

reactions
{
    species
    (
        CH4
        O2
        H2O
        CO2
        N2
    );

    methaneReaction
    {
        type        irreversibleArrheniusReaction;
        reaction    "CH4 + 2O2 = CO2 + 2H2O";
        A           5.012e11;
        beta        0;
        Ta          24358;
    }
};

odeCoeffs
{

<Review_Analysis>
# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is clear:

```
Unknown mixture type reactingMixture
Supported mixture types: 
4
(
pureMixture 
coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture 
multiComponentMixture 
valueMultiComponentMixture 
)
```

This indicates that in **OpenFOAM 10** (as seen from the path `/OpenFOAM-10/`), the mixture type `reactingMixture` is **no longer supported**. The Foundation version (OpenFOAM 10) restructured how reacting mixtures work — the old-style `reactingMixture` has been replaced.

## Root Cause

Looking at the `physicalProperties` file, the `mixture` entry is set to `reactingMixture`, which doesn't exist in OF10. Additionally, the thermophysical properties setup uses `hePsiThermo` with `specie` and `equationOfState perfectGas`, which may also have changed naming conventions.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Fix `physicalProperties` — Change mixture type

In `constant/physicalProperties`, change:
```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```
to:
```
mixture         multiComponentMixture;
```

Then update the thermo-type sub-dictionary accordingly. In OpenFOAM 10, the `hePsiThermo` model expects `multiComponentMixture` when dealing with multiple gas species (a reacting mixture).

Additionally, verify that the `specie` entry is still valid under the new framework — it should remain as-is since `specie` is a separate property.

### 2. Verify `thermo.compressibleGas` consistency

Since `physicalProperties` already references `<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas` as the thermo file, ensure that file is consistent with the new mixture type. The current `thermo.compressibleGas` file duplicates much of `physicalProperties`. It should contain only the species thermodynamic data (which it does correctly), but make sure it doesn't conflict with the updated `physicalProperties`.

### Summary

The primary fix is changing `mixture reactingMixture;` to `mixture multiComponentMixture;` in `constant/physicalProperties`. This aligns with OpenFOAM 10's supported mixture types (`multiComponentMixture` being one of them) and resolves the unknown mixture type error. All other configuration files appear correct for the counterflow flame setup described in the user requirement.
</Review_Analysis>
</Attempt>

<Review_Analysis>
# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword species is undefined in dictionary ".../constant/physicalProperties"
```

This means that in **OpenFOAM 10**, the `physicalProperties` file needs a `species` keyword/list, but the current file doesn't include one. Looking at the history, the previous attempt added `multiComponentMixture` to fix the `reactingMixture` issue, but now the `physicalProperties` file is missing the required `species` entry that OpenFOAM 10 expects to find there.

In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), the `physicalProperties` file must contain a `species` entry listing all species present in the mixture, because `multiComponentMixture` reads this list to know which species coefficients to load.

## Root Cause

The `physicalProperties` file currently contains the `thermoType`, `inertSpecie`, `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, `foamChemistryThermoFile`, and individual species thermodynamic data blocks — but it lacks the top-level `species` keyword that tells `multiComponentMixture` which species are defined.

## Proposed Solution

Add the `species` keyword to the root level of `constant/physicalProperties`. The file should include something like:

```
species
(
    CH4
    O2
    H2O
    CO2
    N2
);
```

This `species` list should be placed near the top of the file, before the `thermoType` sub-dictionary, so that `multiComponentMixture` can parse it and properly initialize the mixture components.

The complete `physicalProperties` file should look like:

```
FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    location    "constant";
    object      physicalProperties;
}

species
(
    CH4
    O2
    H2O
    CO2
    N2
);

thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}

inertSpecie N2;

chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;

foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";

foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";
...
```

This adds the missing `species` keyword while preserving all existing functionality. The rest of the configuration files (boundary conditions, schemes, etc.) appear correct based on the user requirements and previous fixes.
</Review_Analysis>
</Attempt>

<Review_Analysis>
# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword CH4 is undefined in dictionary ".../constant/physicalProperties"
```

This means that after fixing the `species` keyword in Attempt 2, the `physicalProperties` file now requires a `CH4` sub-dictionary (and presumably entries for all listed species), but the current file structure places the species thermodynamic data inside `physicalProperties` rather than in the dedicated `thermo.compressibleGas` file.

Looking at the current `physicalProperties` file, it contains the species thermodynamic data blocks (CH4, O2, H2O, CO2, N2) directly within the main dictionary. However, in **OpenFOAM 10** with `multiComponentMixture`, the `physicalProperties` file itself should NOT contain the species thermodynamic data — those belong in the referenced `thermo.compressibleGas` file. The `physicalProperties` file should only define the mixture type, inert specie, and point to the chemistry/thermo files.

## Root Cause

The `physicalProperties` file has the species thermodynamic data embedded directly in it (the CH4 { ... }, O2 { ... } blocks), but OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` expects these to be loaded from the external `thermo.compressibleGas` file via the `foamChemistryThermoFile` directive. When `multiComponentMixture` tries to look up `CH4` as a sub-dictionary, it can't find it in `physicalProperties` because the reader has already delegated species loading to the thermo file.

## Proposed Solution

Remove the species thermodynamic data blocks (the entire CH4 {...}, O2 {...}, H2O {...}, CO2 {...}, N2 {...} sections) from `constant/physicalProperties`. The file should only contain:

```
FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    location    "constant";
    object      physicalProperties;
}

species
(
    CH4
    O2
    H2O
    CO2
    N2
);

thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}

inertSpecie N2;

chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;

foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";

foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";
```

The actual species thermodynamic data (with `specie`, `thermodynamics`, and `transport` sub-dictionaries for each gas) belongs exclusively in `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`. That file already contains the correct species data blocks, so no changes are needed there. Only `physicalProperties` needs pruning of the embedded species data.

Alternatively, if keeping the species data in `physicalProperties` is preferred, then `foamChemistryThermoFile` should be removed from `physicalProperties` and the thermo file path should be handled differently — but the cleaner approach is to let `thermo.compressibleGas` handle the species data and keep `physicalProperties` minimal as shown above.
</Review_Analysis>
</Attempt>

<Review_Analysis>
# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword CH4 is undefined in dictionary ".../constant/physicalProperties"
```

This occurs because in **OpenFOAM 10** with `multiComponentMixture`, the `physicalProperties` file acts as a container where the mixture looks up species sub-dictionaries. Since we've already tried placing the species thermodynamic data directly in `physicalProperties` (Attempt 3) and removing it (Attempt 2), the current state likely has the `species` list but is missing the individual species sub-dictionaries.

However, looking at the current files provided, the `physicalProperties` file **does not contain** the species thermodynamic data blocks anymore (they were removed per Attempt 3), and it **only** has the `species` list plus the `thermoType` and other entries. The `thermo.compressibleGas` file contains the species data blocks.

The problem is that in OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture`, the `physicalProperties` file itself needs to contain the species sub-dictionaries (not just the `species` list), OR the thermo reading mechanism isn't properly triggering from `thermo.compressibleGas`.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10, when using `multiComponentMixture` and referencing a `thermo.compressibleGas` file via `foamChemistryThermoFile`, the mixture loader should automatically read the species data from that file. But the error suggests that `physicalProperties` is acting as a self-contained dictionary and cannot find `CH4` as a sub-dictionary within itself.

## Proposed Solution

Instead of relying on the `foamChemistryThermoFile` mechanism to pull species data into `physicalProperties`, place the species thermodynamic data blocks **directly inside** `constant/physicalProperties`. Remove the same data from `thermo.compressibleGas` (since it would be redundant anyway), and keep everything in `physicalProperties`:

The `physicalProperties` file should contain:
- The `species` list
- The `thermoType` sub-dict
- The `inertSpecie` entry
- The `chemistryReader` entries
- **AND** the full species thermodynamic data blocks for CH4, O2, H2O, CO2, N2 (with their `specie`, `thermodynamics`, and `transport` sub-dictionaries)

This way, `multiComponentMixture` finds all required species directly within `physicalProperties` without needing to delegate to another file. The `thermo.compressibleGas` file can remain as-is (it won't cause harm), but the critical data must reside in `physicalProperties`.

If keeping `thermo.compressibleGas` is desired, another approach is to remove the `foamChemistryThermoFile` entry from `physicalProperties` entirely — since the chemistry file already references it, and the thermo file path is absolute, this may not be strictly necessary. But the key fix is ensuring `physicalProperties` has the species sub-dictionaries available locally.
</Review_Analysis>
</Attempt>

<Review_Analysis>
# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword reactions is undefined in dictionary ".../constant/chemistryProperties"
```

This means that in **OpenFOAM 10** (Foundation version), the `chemistryProperties` file needs a `reactions` keyword/list, but the current file doesn't have one. The chemistry reader is configured to read reactions from a file, but the reactions themselves need to be specified either directly in `chemistryProperties` or through the chemistry reader mechanism.

Looking at the current `chemistryProperties` file, it contains:
- `chemistryType` with `solver ode; method chemistryModel;`
- `chemistry on;`
- `initialChemicalTimeStep 1e-07;`
- `odeCoeffs` with `seulex` solver settings

But it lacks the `reactions` keyword that tells the chemistry model where to find the reaction definitions.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture` and `hePsiThermo`, the `chemistryProperties` file must either:
1. Contain the `reactions` keyword directly listing the reactions, **OR**
2. Be configured to read reactions from an external file via the chemistry reader

The current file has `chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;` and `foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";` in `physicalProperties`, but `chemistryProperties` itself doesn't reference the reactions file. The chemistry reader in `physicalProperties` handles the reaction parsing, but `chemistryProperties` needs to coordinate with it.

## Proposed Solution

Add the following entries to `constant/chemistryProperties`:

```
chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;

foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";
```

These should be placed at the top level of the `chemistryProperties` dictionary, alongside the existing entries. This tells the chemistry model to use the same foamChemistryReader that was configured in `physicalProperties` to read the reactions from the `<constant>/reactions` file.

The complete `chemistryProperties` file should look like:

```
FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      chemistryProperties;
}

chemistryType
{
    solver          ode;
    method          chemistryModel;
}

chemistry       on;

initialChemicalTimeStep 1e-07;

chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;

foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";

odeCoeffs
{
    solver          seulex;
    absTol          1e-12;
    relTol          1e-01;
}
```

This adds the two missing entries (`chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`) that were previously only present in `physicalProperties`. These entries enable the chemistry model to locate and parse the reaction definitions from the designated file, resolving the "keyword reactions is undefined" error.
</Review_Analysis>
</Attempt>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword reactions is undefined in dictionary ".../constant/chemistryProperties"
```

This is the same error as Attempt 6, meaning the `reactions` keyword is still missing from `chemistryProperties`. Previous attempts added `chemistryReader` and `foamChemistryFile` to `chemistryProperties`, but the chemistry model still couldn't find the reactions.

Looking at the current `chemistryProperties` file, it now contains:
- `chemistryType` with `method chemistryModel;`
- `chemistry on;`
- `initialChemicalTimeStep 1e-07;`
- `chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;`
- `foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";`
- `odeCoeffs` with `seulex` solver settings

Yet the error persists. The issue is that in **OpenFOAM 10** with `multiComponentMixture`, the chemistry model expects the reactions to be accessible directly within `chemistryProperties` — the `foamChemistryReader` and `foamChemistryFile` directives may not be processed in this context, or the reader mechanism works differently.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` with `hePsiThermo`, the chemistry model needs the `reactions` keyword to be present directly in `chemistryProperties`. Simply having the chemistry reader configured elsewhere (in `physicalProperties`) is not sufficient — the chemistry model operates independently and looks for `reactions` within its own dictionary scope.

## Proposed Solution

Add the `reactions` keyword directly to `constant/chemistryProperties`. Since the reactions are defined in the external `<constant>/reactions` file, use the `chemistryReader` syntax to inline them:

Add the following entries to `chemistryProperties`:

```
chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;

foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";

reactions
{
    species
    (
        CH4
        O2
        H2O
        CO2
        N2
    );

    reactions
    (
        methaneReaction
        {
            type        irreversibleArrheniusReaction;
            reaction    "CH4 + 2O2 = CO2 + 2H2O";
            A           5.012e11;
            beta        0;
            Ta          24358;
        }
    );
}
```

Place these entries after `foamChemistryFile` and before `odeCoeffs`. This explicitly exposes the `reactions` keyword within `chemistryProperties` so the chemistry model can access it directly, regardless of whether the chemistry reader processes it from the external file.

The complete `chemistryProperties` file should end with:

```
chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;

foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";

reactions
{
    species
    (
        CH4
        O2
        H2O
        CO2
        N2
    );

    reactions
    (
        methaneReaction
        {
            type        irreversibleArrheniusReaction;
            reaction    "CH4 + 2O2 = CO2 + 2H2O";
            A           5.012e11;
            beta        0;
            Ta          24358;
        }
    );
}

odeCoeffs
{
    solver          seulex;
    absTol          1e-12;
    relTol          1e-01;
}
```

This ensures the chemistry model finds the `reactions` keyword within its immediate scope, resolving the persistent "undefined" error.
</Review_Analysis>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
Attempt to return primitive entry ITstream : .../constant/chemistryProperties/reactions/species, lines 27-33, ...
as a sub-dictionary
```

This error occurs because the `chemistryProperties` file has been updated across multiple attempts to add `reactions`, `chemistryReader`, and `foamChemistryFile`. Now the `reactions` sub-dictionary contains a `species` list, but the chemistry model parser is encountering the literal `species` keyword sequence and trying to interpret it as a sub-dictionary lookup, which fails.

The fundamental problem is that the `reactions` block in `chemistryProperties` is duplicating the `species` sub-list that already exists at the top level of the `reactions` block. In OpenFOAM 10's chemistry model with `multiComponentMixture`, the `species` list is read once from the top-level `reactions` block, and the individual species thermodynamic data is loaded separately (likely from the thermo file). Having a nested `species` inside `reactions` causes a parsing conflict.

## Root Cause

Across Attempts 6 and 7, the `reactions` block was added to `chemistryProperties` containing both a `species` list and the `reactions` sub-list. The parser encounters the inner `species` keyword and tries to treat it as a sub-dictionary entry, which fails because it's a primitive list.

## Proposed Solution

Remove the duplicated `species` sub-dictionary from inside the `reactions` block in `chemistryProperties`. The `reactions` block should only contain the reaction definitions, not the `species` list. The `species` list at the top level of `chemistryProperties` (if present) or the one in `physicalProperties` should suffice.

The `reactions` sub-dictionary in `chemistryProperties` should look like:

```
reactions
(
    methaneReaction
    {
        type        irreversibleArrheniusReaction;
        reaction    "CH4 + 2O2 = CO2 + 2H2O";
        A           5.012e11;
        beta        0;
        Ta          24358;
    }
);
```

So the complete `chemistryProperties` file should be structured as:

```
FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      chemistryProperties;
}

chemistryType
{
    solver          ode;
    method          chemistryModel;
}

chemistry       on;

initialChemicalTimeStep 1e-07;

chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;

foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";

reactions
(
    species
    (
        CH4
        O2
        H2O
        CO2
        N2
    );

    reactions
    (
        methaneReaction
        {
            type        irreversibleArrheniusReaction;
            reaction    "CH4 + 2O2 = CO2 + 2H2O";
            A           5.012e11;
            beta        0;
            Ta          24358;
        }
    );
)

odeCoeffs
{
    solver          seulex;
    absTol          1e-12;
    relTol          1e-01;
}
```

Wait — actually, looking more carefully at the current files, the `species` list appears to be at the top level of `chemistryProperties` already (lines 12-17 in the provided content show `species (...)` outside any sub-dictionary). The issue is that when copying the `reactions` block from the reference tutorial format, the `species` list inside `reactions` conflicts with the top-level one.

Actually, re-reading the error more carefully: it says `reactions/species` — meaning the parser is looking inside the `reactions` sub-dictionary for `species`. This confirms that the `reactions` block contains its own `species` list, which is the problem.

**The fix:** Remove the `species` sub-dictionary from INSIDE the `reactions` block. Keep only the `reactions` sub-list inside `reactions`. The top-level `species` list in `chemistryProperties` (if present) or the one in `physicalProperties` will be found by the chemistry model separately.

So the `reactions` block should be:

```
reactions
(
    species
    (
        CH4
        O2
        H2O
        CO2
        N2
    );

    reactions
    (
        methaneReaction
        {
            type        irreversibleArrheniusReaction;
            reaction    "CH4 + 2O2 = CO2 + 2H2O";
            A           5.012e11;
            beta        0;
            Ta          24358;
        }
    );
)
```

becomes:

```
reactions
(
    methaneReaction
    {
        type        irreversibleArrheniusReaction;
        reaction    "CH4 + 2O2 = CO2 + 2H2O";
        A           5.012e11;
        beta        0;
        Ta          24358;
    }
);
```

The `species` list should exist only at the top level of `chemistryProperties` (where it already is in the provided file), or be omitted entirely since `multiComponentMixture` will get the species list from `physicalProperties`. The key point is: **no `species` keyword inside the `reactions` sub-dictionary.**

<Review_Analysis>
# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
Attempt to return primitive entry ITstream : .../constant/chemistryProperties/reactions, lines 25-34, ...
as a sub-dictionary
```

This means the chemistry model is trying to interpret the `reactions` block as a sub-dictionary, but it's finding a list format `( ... )` instead of a proper dictionary `{ ... }`. The `reactions` block contains entries like `species (...)`, `reactions (...)`, and the individual reaction definition — all as lists — but the parser expects a dictionary structure.

Looking at the current `chemistryProperties` file, the `reactions` block mixes list syntax `( ... )` with dictionary-like entries. In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` with `hePsiThermo`, the `reactions` keyword expects a **dictionary** format, not a list format.

## Root Cause

The `reactions` block in `chemistryProperties` was constructed based on older OpenFOAM tutorials or syntax, using list notation `( ... )` for compound entries. In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), the chemistry model expects the `reactions` entry to be a dictionary where each reaction is named and enclosed in curly braces `{}`.

## Proposed Solution

Rewrite the `reactions` block in `constant/chemistryProperties` to use proper dictionary syntax. Replace the current `reactions` block:

```
reactions
(
    species
    (
        CH4
        O2
        H2O
        CO2
        N2
    );

    reactions
    (
        methaneReaction
        {
            type        irreversibleArrheniusReaction;
            reaction    "CH4 + 2O2 = CO2 + 2H2O";
            A           5.012e11;
            beta        0;
            Ta          24358;
        }
    );
)
```

With a proper dictionary format:

```
reactions
{
    species
    (
        CH4
        O2
        H2O
        CO2
        N2
    );

    methaneReaction
    {
        type        irreversibleArrheniusReaction;
        reaction    "CH4 + 2O2 = CO2 + 2H2O";
        A           5.012e11;
        beta        0;
        Ta          24358;
    }
};
```

Key changes:
1. Wrap the entire `reactions` block in curly braces `{}` instead of parentheses `(())`.
2. Change `reactions ( ... )` to `methaneReaction { ... }` inside the block.
3. Keep `species ( ... )` as a list — this is fine since it's a flat list of keywords.

The complete `chemistryProperties` file should have:

```
FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      chemistryProperties;
}

chemistryType
{
    solver          ode;
    method          chemistryModel;
}

chemistry       on;

initialChemicalTimeStep 1e-07;

chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;

foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";

reactions
{
    species
    (
        CH4
        O2
        H2O
        CO2
        N2
    );

    methaneReaction
    {
        type        irreversibleArrheniusReaction;
        reaction    "CH4 + 2O2 = CO2 + 2H2O";
        A           5.012e11;
        beta        0;
        Ta          24358;
    }
};

odeCoeffs
{
    solver          seulex;
    absTol          1e-12;
    relTol          1e-01;
}
```

This dictionary-style formatting matches what OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` chemistry model expects, and should resolve the "attempt to return primitive entry as sub-dictionary" error.
